question_title: Will NOAA report that March 2026 was the warmest March on record for global surface temperature?
summary: March 2026 was NOT the warmest March on record. Preliminary Copernicus/ERA5 data (reported by Prof. Eliot Jacobson on April 2, 2026) shows March 2026 at +1.48°C above pre-industrial, making it the 4th warmest March on record — 0.20°C below the March 2024 record of +1.68°C. This is consistent with all months in 2026 ranking 4th-5th warmest for their respective months due to La Niña cooling. The 0.20°C gap is far too large for any dataset differences between Copernicus and NOAA to overcome. The NOAA report hasn't been published yet but will almost certainly rank March 2026 well below the record.

factors.description: Preliminary Copernicus/ERA5 data shows March 2026 at +1.48°C above pre-industrial, ranked 4th warmest March — 0.20°C below the March 2024 record of +1.68°C (per Eliot Jacobson, April 2, 2026)
All months in 2026 (Jan, Feb) ranked ~5th warmest for their respective months, consistent with La Niña keeping 2026 well below 2024 record levels
Weak La Niña conditions active through early 2026, pulling global temperatures below El Niño-boosted 2024 levels
NOAA and Copernicus rankings are highly correlated — if Copernicus ranks March 2026 as 4th warmest, NOAA will almost certainly NOT rank it as warmest
NOAA report not yet published (404) but data already collected; publication expected mid-April

reasoning:
**Forecast: 2% probability (NO)**

March 2026 was almost certainly NOT the warmest March on record. The key evidence:

- **Preliminary data**: Prof. Eliot Jacobson reported on April 2, 2026 that March 2026 finished at +1.48°C above pre-industrial baseline, ranking it the **4th warmest March on record**
- **Record holder**: March 2024 holds the record at +1.68°C above pre-industrial — a **0.20°C gap** that is far too large to be bridged by dataset differences
- **Pattern consistency**: January and February 2026 both ranked 5th warmest for their respective months — 2026 is running well below record levels across the board
- **La Niña effect**: Weak La Niña conditions through early 2026 have been suppressing global temperatures compared to the El Niño-boosted 2024 record year
- **NOAA report**: Not yet published (expected mid-April), but NOAA and Copernicus rankings are highly correlated, so NOAA will also not rank it as #1
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will NOAA report that March 2026 was the warmest March on record for global surface temperature?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43050)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if NOAA’s Global Climate Report for March 2026 at https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/access/monitoring/monthly-report/global/202603 states that March 2026 was the warmest March on record globally. Otherwise, it resolves to no.
